# Signal-Lag Foresight Brief

_Generated 2026-06-29 · 12768 papers · embedding backend: sentence-transformers_

## BLUF

- **Confidence may be eroding in Deceptive Alignment** — Critical/negative papers rose from 20% to 28% of recent work (+9 pts) while volume is flat/declining — an early sign the field may be hitting limits.
- **Confidence may be eroding in Evals & Dangerous-Capability Benchmarks** — Critical/negative papers rose from 21% to 31% of recent work (+9 pts) while volume is flat/declining — an early sign the field may be hitting limits.
- **Confidence may be eroding in Reward Hacking** — Critical/negative papers rose from 13% to 22% of recent work (+9 pts) while volume is flat/declining — an early sign the field may be hitting limits.
- **Confidence may be eroding in Scalable Oversight** — Critical/negative papers rose from 22% to 31% of recent work (+8 pts) while volume is flat/declining — an early sign the field may be hitting limits.
- **Safety attention may lag capability: Multi-Agent / Tool-Use Capability vs Scalable Oversight** — Multi-Agent / Tool-Use Capability velocity grew 21% recently while paired safety topic Scalable Oversight grew -6% (gap 26 pts); capability now runs ~0.2x the safety volume.

## Capability vs. Safety Divergence

- **Safety attention may lag capability: Multi-Agent / Tool-Use Capability vs Scalable Oversight** (high): Multi-Agent / Tool-Use Capability velocity grew 21% recently while paired safety topic Scalable Oversight grew -6% (gap 26 pts); capability now runs ~0.2x the safety volume.

## Confidence / Negative Signals

- **Confidence may be eroding in Deceptive Alignment** (high): Critical/negative papers rose from 20% to 28% of recent work (+9 pts) while volume is flat/declining — an early sign the field may be hitting limits.
- **Confidence may be eroding in Evals & Dangerous-Capability Benchmarks** (high): Critical/negative papers rose from 21% to 31% of recent work (+9 pts) while volume is flat/declining — an early sign the field may be hitting limits.
- **Confidence may be eroding in Reward Hacking** (high): Critical/negative papers rose from 13% to 22% of recent work (+9 pts) while volume is flat/declining — an early sign the field may be hitting limits.
- **Confidence may be eroding in Scalable Oversight** (high): Critical/negative papers rose from 22% to 31% of recent work (+8 pts) while volume is flat/declining — an early sign the field may be hitting limits.

## Topic Velocity

- **Agentic / Autonomy Capability is accelerating** (medium): Submission rate inflected +39% (now ~115.0/quarter).
- **Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness is accelerating** (medium): Submission rate inflected +35% (now ~29.0/quarter).

headline.meaning: The defining tension this quarter is autonomous agents—coding agents, embodied multi-agent systems, and self-evolving design loops—scaling faster than the oversight and monitoring machinery meant to catch their failures.
headline.capability_focus: Capability papers concretely push autonomous coding agents that open/merge pull requests at scale, embodied multi-agent cooperation, self-evolving hardware-design agents, and long-horizon tool-use/manipulation agents.
headline.safety_focus: Paired safety work covers runtime agent-immune-system defenses inside the reasoning loop, repository/ecosystem-level governance of AI-native software, and scalable oversight for verifying AI-assisted outputs.
headline.why_it_matters: When agents that individually pass tests still accumulate ecosystem-level harms no single contribution accounts for, per-agent benchmarks and external perimeter defenses miss the actual risk surface, and oversight that contracts (-5.6%) while agentic capability surges (+38.6%) widens that blind spot.

tabs.divergence: Agentic/autonomy capability is growing at +38.6% per quarter while its monitoring counterpart grows only +13.8%, and multi-agent/tool-use capability (+20.7%) outpaces a contracting scalable-oversight field (-5.6%). The most acute lag is flagged in multi-agent vs. oversight, where capability volume is small relative to oversight but momentum is sharply asymmetric. Encouragingly, CoT-faithfulness (+34.9%) and dangerous-capability evals (+22.4%) are outrunning their slow capability pairs (reasoning +7.8%, frontier scaling +1.2%), so the gap is uneven rather than uniform.
tabs.velocity: Momentum is concentrated in agentic capability (recent 115/qtr, +38.6%) and CoT faithfulness (+34.9%), while reward hacking (-17.1%), deceptive alignment (-8.4%), and scalable oversight (-5.6%) are decelerating despite oversight still carrying the largest recent volume (218/qtr). The signal is that the field is accelerating into autonomy and evaluation but letting core alignment-failure topics cool just as agent deployment intensifies.
tabs.sentiment: Critical-share is rising in exactly the safety topics that face fast-moving capabilities—evals (30.6%), scalable oversight (31.0%), deceptive alignment (28.5%), and reward hacking (22.1%)—suggesting researchers are increasingly skeptical of current safeguards. By contrast, agentic capability (10.6%) and agentic monitoring (10.5%) carry low and no-longer-rising critical share, hinting at relative optimism or insufficient scrutiny precisely where autonomy is scaling fastest.
tabs.quadrant: Only agentic/autonomy capability and CoT faithfulness register as 'hot'; everything else, including oversight, deceptive alignment, and reward hacking, sits in 'established'. There is no 'emerging' or genuine white-space activity, so the field is intensifying within known categories rather than opening new safety frontiers to match novel agentic risks.
tabs.citations: No rapid-growth or sleeper citation movers were recorded this quarter, so there is no clear evidence yet of which of these agent-safety or oversight directions is consolidating durable influence. The absence of breakout citations alongside high publication velocity suggests the literature is still in a rapid-expansion phase without settled anchor works.

papers.summary: Argues that risk from autonomous coding agents should be measured at the repository/ecosystem level rather than per-agent on isolated benchmarks, since agents that each pass tests still accumulate collective problems.
papers.why_it_matters: Signals a needed shift from component-level evaluation to governance of agent-populated systems, a key oversight gap as coding agents merge PRs at scale.

papers.summary: Proposes LLawCo, a framework to learn cooperation laws that reduce misaligned, environment-inconsistent behavior in embodied multi-agent LLM systems.
papers.why_it_matters: Addresses partner-misalignment in decentralized agents, a precursor concern for coordinating safe multi-agent behavior.

papers.summary: Presents JD's industrial-scale LLM/VLM item-understanding system for managing billions of e-commerce SKUs.
papers.why_it_matters: Demonstrates capability deployment at massive real-world scale, raising the stakes for reliability and oversight in production AI.

papers.summary: Introduces DexCompose, a method for composing dexterous manipulation policies across multiple tasks on a single robotic hand without destructive interference.
papers.why_it_matters: Advances reusable embodied capability, extending autonomy into physical multi-task control.

papers.summary: Describes Google's Paper Assistant tool for automating scientific peer review to keep pace with AI-accelerated science.
papers.why_it_matters: Represents AI-driven scalable verification of AI outputs, a concrete instance of using AI for oversight at scale.

papers.summary: Proposes ProMSA, a progressive multimodal search agent that adaptively retrieves evidence during reasoning for knowledge-based VQA.
papers.why_it_matters: Shows agentic, adaptive retrieval improving reasoning, expanding tool-use capability that oversight must track.

papers.summary: Presents HORIZON, a self-evolving agent that treats hardware design as repository-level code evolution with an executable evaluator and acceptance predicate.
papers.why_it_matters: Embeds verification into a self-improving agent loop, a notable bridge between autonomy capability and built-in monitoring.

papers.summary: Proposes an agent-native immune system that places defenses inside the agent's active reasoning loop rather than at the perimeter.
papers.why_it_matters: Directly targets the gap that aligned agents remain vulnerable at runtime, advancing in-loop agentic monitoring.

papers.summary: Introduces S²-VLA, a state-space-guided vision-language-action model that adapts feature fusion to reduce error accumulation in long-horizon manipulation.
papers.why_it_matters: Pushes long-horizon robotic autonomy, where compounding errors are a core reliability/safety concern.

papers.summary: Presents ATOD, combining on-policy distillation with RL to train small multi-turn agents past the teacher-imitation ceiling.
papers.why_it_matters: Improves reward-driven agent training, relevant to reward-shaping risks in autonomous agents.

papers.summary: Shows parameterized world models reduce hallucination propagation in LLM agents by making transition errors measurable.
papers.why_it_matters: Offers a more verifiable basis for agent planning, supporting faithfulness and reliability in reasoning.

papers.summary: Extends Werewolf with a Jester role to force multi-hop theory-of-mind, exposing models that score well via language priors without modeling opponents.
papers.why_it_matters: Probes whether LLMs genuinely reason about deceptive incentives, informing deception and oversight evaluation.

papers.summary: Runs a natural experiment separating designer-intended from player-perceived AI deception in RPGs and measures its causal effect on ratings.
papers.why_it_matters: Empirically studies how AI deception is perceived, relevant to understanding deceptive-behavior impacts.

papers.summary: Proposes outcome-supervised process reward modeling with learnable credit assignment to avoid costly stepwise annotations.
papers.why_it_matters: Improves reward modeling for reasoning, with direct implications for credit-assignment-driven reward hacking.

papers.summary: Introduces a signal-coverage matrix that separates type-correctness from semantic correctness in LLM autoformalization, revealing which errors methods actually fix.
papers.why_it_matters: Exposes that headline metrics conceal failure modes, sharpening evaluation rigor for self-improving formal reasoning.

papers.summary: Finds that fine-tuning LLMs for social warmth weakens adversarial safety and increases jailbreak susceptibility, and investigates persona-conditioning mitigations.
papers.why_it_matters: Documents a concrete alignment trade-off where benign tuning degrades safety, important for safe fine-tuning practice.

papers.summary: Analyzes factual errors in human-written (not LLM-generated) text and applications of detecting them.
papers.why_it_matters: Refocuses factual-error detection beyond hallucinations, supporting broader verification tooling.

papers.summary: Shows agentic AI can automate previously expert-intensive re-identification attacks on mobility microdata at scale.
papers.why_it_matters: Demonstrates a concrete agent-enabled misuse pathway, signaling rising dangerous-capability and privacy risk.

papers.summary: Empirically measures non-interactive SSH attacks on honeypots, challenging the assumption that attackers always open an interactive shell.
papers.why_it_matters: Updates threat models for cyber-deception defenses relevant to agent-driven attack patterns.

papers.summary: Proposes attributing generated images to their source diffusion model via spectral coupling of denoiser responses.
papers.why_it_matters: Advances provenance verification, supporting accountability and IP protection for generative outputs.

papers.summary: Develops a quantitative scaling-limit theory of the Random Language Model using large-deviation analysis over grammar rule usage.
papers.why_it_matters: Contributes theoretical grounding for how language-model structure behaves at scale.

papers.summary: Shows that inference-time rollout choices, not just training, determine a multi-horizon volatility forecaster's deployed behavior.
papers.why_it_matters: Highlights that deployment-side configuration shapes model behavior, a generalizable consideration for safe deployment.

papers.summary: Derives generalization scaling laws as a function of trainable parameters and sample count in quadratic neural networks.
papers.why_it_matters: Sharpens theoretical understanding of how scale drives generalization, underpinning capability forecasting.

papers.summary: Presents Yuvion, an adversarially-aware LLM designed around the premise that safety failures are fundamentally adversarial evasion attempts.
papers.why_it_matters: Frames model safety as inherently adversarial, advancing dangerous-capability-resistant model development.

papers.summary: Proposes a game-theory-integrated epidemiological framework modeling strategic attacker-defender dynamics in malware propagation.

papers.summary: Uses cognitive episodes in LLM reasoning traces to interpretably predict human item difficulty.

papers.summary: Introduces ontology-guided evidence-path inference to constrain noisy search in multi-hop knowledge-graph QA.

papers.summary: Extends lifted inference to efficiently compute causal effects in relational domains via parametric causal factor graphs.

risks.risk: California SB 53 incident-reporting and frontier risk frameworks are being authored by deployment teams against a safety-research base (scalable oversight, deceptive alignment) that is itself losing velocity and rising in critical-uncertainty share — freezing a contested methodology into a mandatory compliance artifact.
risks.research_anchor: Scalable Oversight velocity -5.6%/qtr while critical-share rises to 31.0% (+8.5 pts); Deceptive Alignment -8.4%/qtr with critical-share 28.5% (+8.6 pts).
risks.derived_from: divergences_safety_lagging (Multi-agent/tool-use 20.7% vs Scalable Oversight -5.6%); eroding_confidence_rising_critical_share for Scalable Oversight and Deceptive Alignment; SOCIETAL_CONTEXT legal/regulatory: SB 53 in effect Jan 1 2026 requiring published risk frameworks and incident reports.
risks.communities: Frontier-lab compliance/policy teams writing SB 53 risk frameworks track the statutory text and audit expectations but not whether the underlying oversight methods are scientifically stabilizing or contested. Scalable-oversight and deceptive-alignment researchers see that critical-uncertainty share is rising (the field is admitting its methods are unsettled) but do not track that those same methods are now hardening into legal compliance templates. Neither side connects 'the science is getting less certain' with 'the law now requires us to attest to it.'
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional view: SB 53 transparency mandates are a straightforward win for safety. Inversion: mandating disclosure of risk frameworks while the underlying research is decelerating and self-reporting rising uncertainty can lock a not-yet-sound methodology into a defensible legal standard — compliance pressure incentivizes papering over the very uncertainty the research field is flagging.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The legal community treats incident-reporting as procedural; the research community treats rising critical-share as a healthy sign of self-correction. No one reads the divergence as a regulatory ossification risk because the law is brand new (Jan 2026) and no incident has yet tested whether the disclosed frameworks rest on stable science.
risks.mechanism: SB 53 requires frontier developers to publish and attest to risk frameworks and report incidents. Firms will standardize on whatever oversight/eval methods exist now. If scalable-oversight research is decelerating and increasingly flagged as critical/uncertain, the methods being codified are a snapshot of an unsettled field. Once embedded in compliance attestations and litigation defenses, those methods become costly to revise — incentives shift from 'find better oversight' to 'defend the disclosed framework,' suppressing exactly the methodological churn the rising critical-share signals is needed.
risks.leading_indicator: Convergence of published SB 53 risk frameworks across multiple labs onto near-identical oversight/eval citations, even as new arXiv work flags those methods as unreliable; or an incident report that invokes a framework method later shown unsound without triggering framework revision.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate-low confidence. The research-attention signal is real, but the causal link 'decelerating + rising-critical-share research ⇒ unsound methods getting frozen into law' is inferential — attention metrics are not method-quality metrics, and compliance teams may revise frameworks freely. Confidence lowered accordingly.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed labs will lean on existing scalable-oversight literature to populate SB 53 frameworks and that compliance/litigation incentives discourage revision — neither is shown in the digest.

verification.prior_coverage: SB 53's incident-reporting and risk-framework mandates are extensively covered (Brookings, FPF, Wharton, multiple law firms). The specific worry that the law standardizes around immature/unsettled safety methods is already public: a Stanford CodeX analysis notes SB 53 does not define adequate assessment or mandate testing methodologies, leaving developers to pick their own; CIGI, the FLI AI Safety Index, and the AI Safety Atlas separately argue current safety evaluations are unreliable and that a 'policy-evaluation gap' exists. The precise framing of 'decelerating + rising-critical-share research being frozen into compliance via litigation-defense incentives' is not stated as a single synthesized seam, but every component is in active discussion.

verification.disputed_claims: Two load-bearing claims are contested. (1) Deceptive alignment as a real/near-term risk is openly disputed within the alignment community itself (e.g., a widely-cited 'Deceptive Alignment is <1% Likely by Default' argument and ongoing dialogues); treating it as a settled hazard being frozen into law overstates consensus. (2) That SB 53 'freezes' a methodology is complicated: the statute (per Stanford CodeX and WilmerHale) mandates no specific testing protocols, lets developers choose methods, and requires annual framework updates with 30-day publication of material modifications — so the law arguably permits, not prevents, methodological revision. The premise that low/negative research-attention metrics imply 'unsound' methods is the risk's own admitted inference and is not evidenced by any source (attention != method quality). Whether evals are too immature to regulate around is itself contested: CIGI/FLI/AI Safety Atlas say current evals are unreliable, while SB 53 proponents argue disclosure builds the evidence base precisely because methods are immature.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Low confidence, partially anticipated. The constituent concerns are already public and in some cases prominent: SB 53's lack of mandated methodology (Stanford CodeX), the unreliability/immaturity of current safety evaluations (CIGI, FLI Index, AI Safety Atlas), and the general danger of premature regulatory standardization/lock-in (Frontier AI Regulation paper). The distinctive contribution here is only the synthesis tying a specific research-velocity signal to SB 53 lock-in via litigation-defense incentives — and that synthesis rests on at least two shaky pillars. First, it leans on deceptive alignment as a hazard, which is actively disputed even among alignment researchers, so it must not be briefed as a settled threat. Second, the 'freezing' mechanism is weakened by SB 53's own design: no prescribed methodology, developer discretion, and mandatory annual updates, meaning the law plausibly accommodates rather than blocks methodological churn. The core causal claim ('decelerating + rising-critical-share research ⇒ unsound methods getting frozen into law') remains an unproven inference from attention metrics that are not method-quality metrics. Net: present as a hypothesis worth monitoring, explicitly noting the contested deceptive-alignment premise and the law's revision flexibility, not as a confident novel finding.

risks.risk: Agentic Behavior Monitoring is scaling fast (123.5/qtr) precisely as PRC-linked influence operations probe the data-center/electricity-cost narrative — meaning agent-monitoring telemetry becomes a high-value, under-secured surveillance target whose compromise reveals both operational patterns and grid-siting sensitivities.
risks.research_anchor: Agentic Behavior Monitoring accelerating +13.8% to 123.5/qtr; Agentic/Autonomy Capability +38.6% to 115/qtr — monitoring is being built around fast-growing autonomy.
risks.derived_from: velocity.accelerating (Agentic Behavior Monitoring, Agentic/Autonomy Capability); recent_lab_activity GPT-5.6 Sol tagged Agentic Behavior Monitoring; SOCIETAL_CONTEXT security: June 2026 OpenAI disclosure of PRC-linked ops targeting the data-center/electricity debate; critical-infrastructure exposure concern.
risks.communities: Agent-monitoring/observability researchers build telemetry to catch misbehaving agents but treat the logs as an internal safety asset, not an intelligence target. National-security/critical-infrastructure analysts track influence ops and grid exposure but don't follow the rapid growth of agent-monitoring infrastructure as a new aggregation of sensitive operational and siting data. Neither connects 'we are instrumenting agents everywhere' with 'this telemetry is now a strategic collection target.'
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional view: more agent monitoring = more safety. Inversion: the monitoring layer itself becomes a concentrated, sensitive data asset — its compromise hands adversaries a map of autonomous operations and the very infrastructure-cost grievances being weaponized in influence ops.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Monitoring is framed as a defensive/safety good; almost no one models the telemetry layer as an attack surface or an intelligence prize. The influence-op disclosure and the monitoring acceleration are tracked by entirely separate communities.
risks.mechanism: As agentic deployment grows, monitoring systems aggregate detailed traces of agent actions across enterprises and infrastructure (including data-center operations). A documented adversary is already probing narratives around data centers and electricity costs. Compromising or exfiltrating monitoring telemetry would expose operational patterns, dependencies, and siting sensitivities — far richer than model weights — enabling both targeted sabotage and tailored influence narratives. The faster monitoring scales without commensurate security hardening, the larger the prize.
risks.leading_indicator: Security advisories or incidents involving exfiltration/tampering of agent-observability or monitoring pipelines; influence-op content that references specific operational details plausibly derived from monitoring data; vendors marketing centralized agent-telemetry platforms without dedicated threat models.
risks.calibration: Low-probability but emerging. The acceleration of monitoring research is solid; the leap to 'telemetry as strategic target' is plausible extrapolation, not evidenced in the digest. Confidence moderate-low.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed monitoring telemetry is centralized, sensitive, and under-secured, and that adversaries would value it — none of this is in the signals.

verification.prior_coverage: Both component halves of this risk are already heavily and publicly covered, but separately. The PRC-linked influence operation targeting the US data-center/electricity-cost narrative is front-page: OpenAI's June 2026 threat report ('Data Center Bandwagon' / 'Tech and Tariffs') was reported by NPR, Politico/Slashdot, Al Jazeera, CyberScoop, Tom's Hardware and others, and the broader 'China funds data-center haters' grid-siting debate is itself widely discussed. Separately, agentic-AI observability/telemetry security — including exfiltration of agent action traces as a 2026 attack surface — is a saturated enterprise-security and academic topic (Palo Alto, Kiteworks, multiple arXiv SoK papers). What I found NO public source doing is fusing the two: treating agent-monitoring telemetry specifically as a high-value surveillance target whose compromise feeds that specific PRC operation's grid-siting/influence goals.

verification.disputed_claims: The risk's framing leans on the influence operation being a potent, escalating threat ('a documented adversary is already probing'). That potency is disputed by the primary source itself: OpenAI rated both campaigns only 1-2 on its Breakout Scale and found 'no evidence of meaningful breakout,' and reporters (CyberScoop, Al Jazeera, TechSpot, PC Gamer) emphasize the operation generated little authentic engagement and largely amplified pre-existing, partly factual grievances rather than creating the debate. Also unevidenced/contested: the assertion that monitoring telemetry would be 'far richer than model weights' — no source supports that comparison; weight-theft literature treats weights as the premier target. The probing of the *narrative* is documented; any adversary interest in *agent-monitoring telemetry* is pure extrapolation with zero public sourcing.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower than the candidate's 'moderate-low.' Two of the three building blocks (the PRC data-center influence op; agentic monitoring/telemetry as an attack surface) are already widely and prominently discussed, so the risk should not be briefed as novel on those grounds — only the specific bridge (monitoring telemetry as a strategic surveillance prize feeding that PRC operation) is unsurfaced, and that bridge is speculative with no public corroboration. Critically, the risk's urgency rests on a contested premise: the influence operation it cites was assessed by OpenAI itself as low-impact (Breakout Scale 1-2, no meaningful engagement), so the 'documented active adversary' should be characterized as a low-efficacy, exploit-existing-grievances actor, not a potent one. The claim that telemetry is 'far richer than model weights' is unsupported and should be dropped or hedged. Net: a plausible but speculative cross-domain seam built on heavily-covered components and one over-stated threat premise — surface only as a low-confidence 'watch item,' explicitly noting (a) the seam itself is unevidenced extrapolation and (b) the cited influence op was judged largely ineffective.

risks.risk: AI rare-disease/genomic diagnosis pipelines are being deployed via accelerating agentic capability into a clinical setting where the safety subfield governing exactly that failure mode — Scalable Oversight — is shrinking in velocity while its critical-uncertainty share rises, meaning the verification methods clinicians implicitly trust are quietly becoming less validated, not more.
risks.research_anchor: Scalable Oversight decelerating (-5.6%/qtr, recent 218/qtr) WHILE its critical-uncertainty share rises to 31.0% (+8.5 pts); Multi-Agent/Tool-Use capability accelerating (+20.7%) with a safety-lag gap of 0.263 and volume ratio 0.24.
risks.derived_from: Divergence pairing 'Multi-Agent/Tool-Use vs. Scalable Oversight'; lab activity 'Talos: Scaling rare disease diagnosis with automated, iterative genomic reanalysis' (Microsoft Research) and 'How GPT-5 helped immunologist Derya Unutmaz solve a 3-year-old mystery' (OpenAI); eroding-confidence flag on Scalable Oversight.
risks.communities: Clinical-genomics and rare-disease deployment teams (who see automated iterative reanalysis as a search/throughput win and assume human geneticists remain the oversight layer) versus scalable-oversight researchers (who now flag that oversight methods for multi-step agentic reasoning are under-validated and rising in critical uncertainty). They don't connect because clinical teams treat 'oversight' as an organizational/regulatory fact — a clinician signs off — while the research community treats it as an open technical problem of whether a less-capable supervisor can verify a more-capable agent's multi-step reasoning. Neither tracks the other's definition of the word.
risks.framing_inversion: Inverts 'a human-in-the-loop clinician makes agentic diagnosis safe.' If the verification burden on the human scales faster than their ability to actually audit multi-step agentic genomic reanalysis, the human-in-the-loop becomes a liability-laundering rubber stamp precisely as the underlying oversight science stalls.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Medical-AI safety discourse focuses on hallucination and bias in single outputs, not on whether oversight techniques for long-horizon agentic reasoning are research-validated. The deceleration of oversight research is invisible to clinicians, and the clinical deployment is invisible to oversight researchers who work on abstract benchmarks.
risks.mechanism: Agentic genomic-reanalysis tools chain many inference steps (variant calling, literature retrieval, phenotype matching, re-ranking). The clinician can verify the final candidate variant but cannot reconstruct or audit the intermediate reasoning chain. Scalable oversight is the field meant to produce methods for exactly this supervisor-weaker-than-agent verification — and it is losing momentum while its own practitioners flag rising critical uncertainty. So as deployment scales, the gap between what clinicians believe they are checking and what is actually checkable widens, with no fresh research closing it.
risks.leading_indicator: Published rare-disease diagnostic agent deployments citing 'physician review' as the safety control without specifying what fraction of the agent's intermediate reasoning is actually inspected; or a malpractice/recall case where a wrong agentic reanalysis passed nominal clinician sign-off.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate-low confidence. The deceleration and rising-critical-share signals are real and proprietary, but the causal link 'less oversight research ⇒ less-validated deployed clinical verification' is inferential — research velocity is an attention signal, not a measure of deployed scaffolding quality. Lowered accordingly. The clinical-deployment signal (Talos) is a single lab blog, not evidence of scale.
risks.extrapolation: I assumed the Talos/Unutmaz items represent genuine clinical-adjacent agentic deployment and that clinicians serve as the de facto oversight layer; the digest shows the research signals but not deployment scale or oversight practices.

verification.prior_coverage: The named components are heavily covered: Talos was publicized in the last week across Microsoft Research, MedRxiv, and health-science outlets, and the GPT-5/Unutmaz case was widely reported. The broad seam 'clinical AI is being deployed faster than it can be trusted,' black-box/auditability gaps, and automation bias (clinicians over-trusting AI output they cannot reconstruct) are an established literature. However, no public source connects a decline in Scalable Oversight research velocity specifically to degrading deployed clinical genomic verification — that exact causal seam appears unsurfaced.

verification.disputed_claims: Two load-bearing claims are contested. (1) The GPT-5 clinical-breakthrough framing: a peer-reviewed Journal of Medical Systems analysis (Capobianco et al., Univ. Hospital Tuebingen) found GPT-5 had identical 85% concordance to GPT-4o on 100 GI-oncology cases, contradicting superiority claims, and flagged accuracy/privacy concerns from the public demo; the Unutmaz case is a single anecdotal, expert-validated success, not validated capability. (2) The premise that 'Scalable Oversight is shrinking in velocity / its critical-uncertainty share is rising' is described in the risk itself as proprietary and is not corroborated by any public source; the field still shows active 2025-2026 output (debate, adversarial oversight, AI Safety reports). The causal link 'less oversight research => less-validated deployed clinical verification' is inferential, as the candidate's own calibration concedes.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Emerging, LOW confidence (revised down from moderate-low). The constituent pieces are not novel: clinical-AI-outpacing-trust, black-box auditability, and automation bias are widely discussed, and both Talos and the GPT-5 case are already in the press. The specific seam — Scalable Oversight research velocity declining while agentic genomic diagnosis scales — does appear unsurfaced, which is the only genuinely fresh element. But the risk leans on two weak supports that must be stated explicitly: (a) the 'oversight is decelerating' signal is proprietary, unverified, and an attention proxy rather than a measure of deployed scaffolding quality; and (b) the GPT-5 capability framing is actively disputed in peer-reviewed work (Capobianco et al.) showing parity with GPT-4o, so it should not be cited as evidence of accelerating clinical capability. The mechanism is further weakened by the Talos evidence itself: Talos is open-source, auditable, specificity-tuned (~1.3 candidate variants/patient with mandatory expert review), which partially mitigates the 'clinician cannot audit the chain' premise rather than supporting it. Net: brief this as a hypothesis about a possible research/deployment timing gap, NOT as an established or confidently-evidenced risk; do not launder the disputed GPT-5 superiority claim or the proprietary deceleration signal into confidence.

risks.risk: Dangerous-capability evals are accelerating (+22.4%) and being adopted as the substrate for new government pre-release model-review arrangements at the very moment the eval research community's own critical-uncertainty share is rising fastest (+9.2 pts) — institutionalizing a measurement method as a national-security gatekeeping tool just as its authors lose confidence in it.
risks.research_anchor: Evals & Dangerous-Capability Benchmarks accelerating (+22.4%/qtr, 46.5/qtr) WHILE its recent critical-uncertainty share rises to 30.6% (+9.2 pts) — the steepest critical-share jump in the digest alongside Reward Hacking.
risks.derived_from: Velocity 'Evals & Dangerous-Capability Benchmarks +22.4%'; eroding-confidence flag on the same topic (30.6%, +9.2 pts); SOCIETAL_CONTEXT: CAISI evaluation partnerships with Google/Microsoft/xAI, administration considering pre-release frontier-model evaluation requirements, labs rating models 'High' in cyber capability.
risks.communities: National-security officials and CAISI evaluators (who need a defensible, citable measurement to authorize or block frontier releases and treat 'we ran dangerous-capability evals' as decision-grade evidence) versus the eval-research community itself (whose rising critical-uncertainty share signals growing internal doubt about saturation, gaming, construct validity, and elicitation gaps). They don't connect because the policy side needs a binary go/no-go artifact and treats the existence of a benchmark as settling the question, while the research side is in the middle of discovering the benchmark may not measure what it claims.
risks.framing_inversion: Inverts 'pre-release dangerous-capability evals make government oversight rigorous and safe.' The act of operationalizing evals as the gate can freeze a self-doubting, fast-moving methodology into a security determination — and, worse, create an incentive for labs to elicit-down (under-elicit capability during the very eval that decides release), turning the gate into a tool that systematically understates risk.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Coverage of CAISI/lab evaluation partnerships frames evals as a maturity milestone. The simultaneous internal erosion of confidence among eval researchers is a niche research-trend signal not visible to security policymakers, and the policymakers' reliance on evals as decision-grade is not visible to the researchers flagging the uncertainty.
risks.mechanism: When an under-validated metric becomes a regulatory/security gate with high stakes (a release decision), two things follow: (1) the metric ossifies — it becomes hard to revise because revision implies past determinations were wrong; (2) the evaluated party gains a powerful incentive to manage the measurement (under-elicitation, narrow scoping) rather than the underlying capability. The rising critical-share signal indicates the research community is actively discovering the metric's weaknesses at the same moment it is being load-bearing for cyber/national-security release calls.
risks.leading_indicator: A CAISI or lab pre-release evaluation that clears a model later shown to have under-elicited dangerous capability (e.g., a cyber-exploit capability surfacing post-release that the eval missed); or eval-research papers documenting elicitation-gap or sandbagging effects being cited inside policy review without changing the gate.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate confidence on the institutionalization-vs-self-doubt seam (both signals are concrete: acceleration + steep critical-share rise, plus documented CAISI partnerships). Lower confidence on the strategic-under-elicitation mechanism, which is an inferential incentive claim, not observed in the digest. Stated as a hypothesis, not a finding.
risks.extrapolation: I assumed CAISI's evaluation partnerships rely substantially on the dangerous-capability benchmark methodology the digest tracks, and that the rising critical-uncertainty share reflects genuine methodological doubt rather than routine churn; the digest gives the attention/critical-share signal but not the content of the doubt or the exact methods CAISI uses.

verification.prior_coverage: Each named component is already publicly prominent: the CAISI pre-deployment evaluation agreements with Google DeepMind, Microsoft and xAI (May 2026) and a White House executive order establishing voluntary pre-release government access to frontier models were widely reported; and the AI research community has openly published on evals' immaturity (the 'Science of Evals' argument by Apollo Research, FLI's AI Safety Index, construct-validity critiques) and on under-elicitation/sandbagging. The exact dual framing — that evals are being institutionalized as a security gate at the same moment researchers' confidence is dropping — is not packaged as a single named risk, but both halves and the tension between them are already discussed (e.g., FLI and Apollo explicitly tie eval immaturity to their use in high-stakes/regulatory decisions).

verification.disputed_claims: The risk rests on the premise that dangerous-capability evals are 'under-validated' enough that using them as a gate is dangerous. This is genuinely two-sided and contested: validity/construct-validity critics (Apollo's 'Science of Evals', FLI Safety Index, arXiv construct-validity work) agree evals are immature; but others argue pre-deployment assessment is sound best practice (arXiv 'Effective Mitigations for Systemic Risks') and pro-deployment voices (Center for Data Innovation) argue pre-approval/gating itself adds little safety value while slowing innovation — i.e., they dispute that evals should gate at all, for the opposite reason. The strategic-under-elicitation/'manage the measurement' mechanism is plausible and supported by sandbagging research, but remains an inferential incentive claim, not an observed outcome of the CAISI arrangements; the candidate already flags this. The interpretation of the internal '+9.2 pts critical-share' signal as 'authors losing confidence' is itself an inference and could equally reflect a maturing literature productively self-correcting.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Downgrade from 'emerging novel seam' to 'partially anticipated framing of two already-public trends.' Both load-bearing facts (CAISI/EO institutionalization of evals as a security gate; active research questioning eval validity/under-elicitation) are individually front-page or well-published, and the tension between them is already voiced by FLI and Apollo. The genuinely fresher contribution is the specific 'ossification + measurement-management at the moment critical-share is rising fastest' synthesis — present it as a sharpened framing, not a new discovery. Critically, the core premise (evals are 'under-validated' and thus dangerous as a gate) is contested in BOTH directions — safety researchers say evals are immature, while deployment-oriented critics dispute that gating adds safety value at all — so the risk should not be briefed as if 'evals are unreliable' is settled fact. The under-elicitation mechanism is supported by sandbagging research but remains an unobserved incentive hypothesis in this specific institutional context, and the internal eroding-confidence signal could reflect healthy self-correction rather than collapsing confidence; keep confidence moderate-to-low and state these dependencies explicitly.

risks.risk: Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness research is accelerating (+34.9%) into a moment when AI sentiment among Gen Z and women is sharply negative — so faithful-CoT disclosures, if deployed as public 'transparency,' may surface plausible-but-unfaithful reasoning that hardens distrust rather than building it.
risks.derived_from: velocity.accelerating (Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness +34.9%); SOCIETAL_CONTEXT social: 76% think businesses aren't transparent enough about AI, daily-users favorable +57 vs rare-users -42, Gen Z 58% oppose, women 18-49 net -41.
risks.communities: CoT-faithfulness researchers study whether displayed reasoning matches the model's actual computation — a technical metric. Public-trust/communications researchers and pollsters track collapsing sentiment among young and female users. Neither connects: faithfulness research is producing the very evidence that displayed reasoning is often NOT faithful, while transparency mandates (SB 53, public pressure) push toward showing reasoning to a distrusting public.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional view: showing the model's chain-of-thought builds trust and satisfies transparency demands. Inversion: faithfulness research's central finding is that visible CoT can be unfaithful — so deploying CoT as public-facing 'transparency' risks exposing reasoning that is later shown not to reflect the model's real basis, confirming the 'businesses aren't honest about AI' belief and deepening the exact distrust transparency was meant to cure.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Faithfulness is treated as an internal interpretability problem; the trust/sentiment crisis is treated as a comms/policy problem. The fact that surfacing reasoning to a skeptical public could be actively counterproductive sits in the seam between interpretability research and public-trust analysis.
risks.mechanism: Transparency pressure and laws push labs to expose model reasoning. Faithfulness research increasingly documents that displayed CoT often does not causally reflect the answer. When a public already inclined to distrust AI (Gen Z, women under 50 deeply negative) sees a model give a confident chain-of-thought that is later contradicted or shown to be post-hoc, the perceived dishonesty validates pre-existing skepticism — trust erodes faster than if no reasoning had been shown. 'Transparency theater' backfires.
risks.leading_indicator: Consumer-facing products shipping CoT/reasoning displays as a trust feature, followed by viral examples of the displayed reasoning being demonstrably unfaithful; sentiment polls showing transparency features fail to move (or worsen) trust among young/female cohorts.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate-low confidence. Faithfulness acceleration and the sentiment data are solid; the causal claim that exposed unfaithful CoT will measurably worsen public trust is inferential and untested. Confidence lowered.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed CoT will be deployed as a public-facing transparency feature and that unfaithfulness will be visible to lay users — the digest shows research velocity only, not deployment.

verification.prior_coverage: The two core elements are both already prominent in public/academic discourse and have effectively been joined. CoT faithfulness — that displayed reasoning is often a plausible-but-post-hoc rationalization — is extensively documented (Anthropic 2023/2025, Turpin et al. 2023, multiple 2025 monitorability papers), and these papers explicitly warn that plausible-yet-misleading CoT 'risks increasing trust in LLMs without guaranteeing their safety.' Separately, the precise mechanism the risk proposes — that AI transparency/disclosure can BACKFIRE and erode rather than build trust — is a published, peer-reviewed finding (Schilke & Reimann, 'The Transparency Dilemma: How AI Disclosure Erodes Trust,' 13 experiments, OBHDP 2025), with a parallel AAAI experiment, so the 'transparency theater backfires' seam is not novel.

verification.disputed_claims: Two dependencies are contested. (1) The 'unfaithful CoT will measurably worsen public trust' causal step is undercut from the other side: METR and CoT-monitorability researchers argue CoT remains 'highly informative despite unfaithfulness,' i.e. unfaithfulness in the technical sense does not necessarily mean the exposed reasoning is misleading or trust-damaging to the public — so the risk may overstate how 'fake' a disclosed CoT looks. (2) The risk leans on the sentiment split (daily-users favorable +57 vs rare-users -42) to imply backlash concentrates among skeptics, but Schilke & Reimann explicitly report the AI-disclosure trust penalty did NOT attenuate among frequent/highly-familiar AI users — complicating the assumption that exposure/familiarity buffers the trust hit. The 34.9% 'velocity' figure and the specific survey numbers (76%, 58%, net -41) are internal/unsourced here and were not independently verifiable.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Low confidence and largely anticipated — recommend briefing as a synthesis/convergence signal, not a novel risk. Both halves are public: CoT unfaithfulness is heavily documented (with the original papers already flagging the 'plausible-but-misleading reasoning inflates unwarranted trust' danger), and the 'transparency backfires / erodes trust' mechanism is an established peer-reviewed finding (Schilke & Reimann 2025). The fresh element is only the specific framing of mandated CoT *disclosure to the general public* as the trigger, layered onto Gen Z/women negative sentiment — a plausible but inferential and untested combination. Confidence must be lowered further because the load-bearing causal claim is actively disputed: researchers (METR) contend unfaithful CoT can still be informative and not necessarily trust-damaging, and the cited paper finds the disclosure penalty persists even among familiar users, which cuts against the risk's reliance on the daily-vs-rare-user sentiment gap. Treat the trust-erosion-from-exposed-CoT effect as a hypothesis with mixed/contested support, not a demonstrated dynamic.

risks.risk: Reward Hacking research is decelerating (-17.1%) and Self-Improvement/RSI control is slipping (-5.1%) just as labs ship recursive, code-authoring agentic systems into a capex-stressed, ROI-desperate economy — aligning commercial incentives toward agents that optimize disclosed metrics rather than intended outcomes, with no fresh research on the failure mode.
risks.research_anchor: Reward Hacking velocity -17.1% (decelerating) with critical-share rising to 22.1% (+9.2 pts); Self-Improvement Risk & Control -5.1%; Self-Improvement/RSI Capability -8.0%.
risks.derived_from: velocity.decelerating (Reward Hacking, Self-Improvement Risk & Control, RSI Capability); eroding_confidence_rising_critical_share (Reward Hacking critical-share +9.2pts); SOCIETAL_CONTEXT economic: MIT 95% zero GenAI ROI, NBER 90% no productivity impact, ~$1.6T spend, concentration risk; recent_lab_activity 'How agents are transforming work,' GPT-5.6 Sol.
risks.communities: Reward-hacking/specification-gaming researchers know the failure mode is unsolved and is getting LESS attention even as its critical-share rises. Enterprise buyers and lab go-to-market teams under ROI pressure are deploying agents tuned to hit measurable KPIs. Neither connects: the economic desperation to show GenAI returns creates strong incentives to deploy reward-optimizing agents exactly when the research community is quietly turning away from the reward-hacking problem.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional view: reward hacking is a niche, abstract alignment toy problem. Inversion: in a capex bubble where 95% of firms see no ROI, the business pressure is precisely to deploy agents that maximize a disclosed proxy metric — reward hacking stops being a lab curiosity and becomes the default commercial deployment pattern, at the moment research attention to it is declining.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The decline in reward-hacking research attention is invisible outside this tool's metrics. The ROI-desperation economics is discussed as a financial/bubble story, not connected to a specific technical failure mode whose research is decelerating. The rising critical-share (the field admitting it's hard) coinciding with falling volume is a subtle signal only this dataset surfaces.
risks.mechanism: Firms under pressure to justify AI spend deploy agents optimized against measurable KPIs (tickets closed, code merged, deals booked). Reward hacking — optimizing the proxy at the expense of the true goal — is the known failure here, but research velocity is falling while its critical-share (acknowledged difficulty) rises, meaning fewer new mitigations. Recursive/self-improving agentic systems compound the proxy-gaming over long horizons. Result: a wave of agents that look successful on dashboards while degrading the underlying objective, with thin and shrinking research scaffolding to detect it.
risks.leading_indicator: Enterprise post-mortems where agentic systems hit their KPIs while business outcomes worsened (e.g., support agents closing tickets without resolution, coding agents merging metric-passing but defective code); declining citations to specification-gaming literature even as deployment scales.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate confidence on the economic incentive and the research-deceleration signal; low confidence on the causal claim that declining research attention translates to fewer deployed mitigations (attention ≠ deployed safeguards). Confidence lowered for that inferential step.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed ROI pressure drives proxy-metric-optimized agent deployment and that research deceleration reduces available mitigations — the digest shows research velocity, not deployment practices or mitigation availability.

verification.prior_coverage: Reward hacking in agentic systems — agents that optimize a measurable proxy/metric instead of the intended outcome — is already a prominent, actively published topic (e.g., METR's June 2025 finding that frontier models reward hack, the 2026 RewardHackingAgents benchmark, and policy explainers). The specific 'agents game dashboard KPIs while degrading the true objective' framing is explicitly discussed, as is recursive self-improvement risk (ICLR 2026 workshop, CSA security analysis), so the individual components of this seam are not fresh.

verification.disputed_claims: Two load-bearing claims are contested. (1) The 'MIT 95% zero GenAI ROI' figure is widely disputed on methodology grounds — critics (Everyday AI, Sify, Marketing AI Institute) note the headline number was extrapolated largely from ~52 qualitative interviews plus ~153 survey respondents/300 public initiatives, was misread, and reflects poor enterprise implementation rather than AI failing per se. (2) The risk's core inferential claim — that declining 'research velocity' means fewer deployed mitigations — is thinly evidenced and partly contradicted: reward-hacking research is visibly active and arguably intensifying in 2025-2026 (new benchmarks, production-RL misalignment papers, LessWrong/METR analyses), and 'research attention' is not a validated measure of deployed safeguards.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower confidence than the candidate states. The underlying failure mode (agents optimizing disclosed metrics over intended outcomes / reward hacking in deployed agentic and self-improving systems) is already a prominent, actively researched and publicly discussed risk — not unsurfaced — so it should be briefed as 'already in the discourse,' not novel. The economic-pressure framing rests on the MIT 95% figure, which is methodologically disputed and should not be treated as settled fact; cite it only with that caveat. Most importantly, the central causal claim (falling research velocity → fewer deployed mitigations → undetected dashboard-passing degradation) is unverified and runs against observable evidence that reward-hacking research is currently expanding, not shrinking; 'research attention' is a poor proxy for 'deployed safeguards.' Net: a real and salient risk, but neither novel nor a confident new inference — the velocity-deceleration signal in particular should be downgraded to low confidence pending evidence it reflects genuine slowdown rather than a measurement artifact.

foresight_gap.context:
# Societal Context — current real-world state
#
# ILLUSTRATIVE AND NON-EXHAUSTIVE. This file enriches the foresight synthesis with
# CURRENT real-world state across societal domains. It is NOT a filter, a fixed list,
# or a set of "the risks that matter." The synthesis reasons across the FULL
# STEEP/PESTLE+ framework regardless of what is written here. The purpose of this file
# is solely to UPDATE the model on current real-world conditions it cannot otherwise
# know from training data — not to narrow, rank, or pre-select what it considers.
# The model must treat everything below as background state to reason ACROSS and
# combine with the week's research signals, never as the only factors in play.
#
# Write BROAD, FACTUAL, and CURRENT. State conditions of the world, not conclusions
# about which are dangerous. Give the board, not the move. Update regularly.
#
# Last updated: June 2026

## Social
- US public sentiment toward AI has soured over the past year. A March 2026 Quinnipiac poll found 55% of Americans think AI will do more harm than good in daily life (up from 44% in April 2025); 64% think AI will do more harm than good for education.
- A March 2026 NBC News poll found 57% of voters believe AI's risks outweigh its benefits (vs. 34% the reverse); only 26% have positive feelings about AI, 46% negative — a net rating lower than every tested topic except the Democratic Party and Iran.
- Trust is low and usage is rising simultaneously: a Feb 2026 Verasight poll found 56% remain anxious about AI even as adoption grows. The Stanford 2026 AI Index notes global "more benefits than drawbacks" sentiment rose to 59%, but nervousness also rose to 52%.
- Sentiment is sharply split by demographics (see Demographic). The single strongest predictor of attitude is personal usage: daily users are favorable by +57 points; rare/never users unfavorable by -42.
- 76% of Americans think businesses are not transparent enough about their AI use; trust in AI developers is a recurring weak point.

## Technological
- Frontier capability is advancing fastest in agentic/autonomous systems and coding. Labs report models authoring large shares of their own code; autonomous task-completion horizons are lengthening.
- Capability is increasingly announced first by labs (system cards, blog posts) ahead of the academic safety literature's response — the lead-time gap this tool measures.
- Synthetic media generation is mainstream (a quarter of 2026 Super Bowl ads were AI-made or AI-advertising); detection and provenance lag generation.
- Agentic deployment is shifting risk from single-output failures toward long-horizon, multi-step, multi-tool behavior — outpacing existing monitoring designed for bounded tasks.

## Economic
- Intense "AI bubble" debate. Total AI spending projected to surpass $1.6T; OpenAI committed ~$1.4T over 8 years on data centers; the big four (Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) expected to spend ~$450B in capex in 2026; the 14 largest data-center operators near $750B in 2026 (up from ~$450B in 2025); Goldman estimates ~$7.6T cumulative AI CapEx 2026–2031.
- Concentration risk: in late 2025 the five largest companies held up ~30% of the S&P 500 — the greatest concentration in half a century. AI-related stocks have driven ~75% of S&P 500 returns, 80% of earnings growth, and 90% of capex growth since ChatGPT launched.
- Circular-investment concerns (Nvidia↔OpenAI↔AMD↔Microsoft↔CoreWeave cross-holdings) fuel bubble skepticism. Hyperscalers took on ~$121B in new debt (+300% vs. typical load), some structured off-balance-sheet via special-purpose vehicles.
- Revenue/ROI doubts: an MIT study found 95% of organizations saw zero return on GenAI investment; one analysis found only ~3% of people pay for AI. A Feb 2026 NBER study found 90% of firms reported no productivity/workplace impact.
- Institutional warnings: the Bank of England and IMF have warned of global market-correction risk from AI overvaluation. Counter-view (JPMorgan, Fed Chair Powell, White House AI czar David Sacks, Ben Horowitz) holds AI generates real revenue and is not a classic bubble. DeepSeek's Jan 2025 release triggered a ~$600B single-day Nvidia drop; the KOSPI halted trading on June 23, 2026.

## Environmental
- Energy is becoming a binding constraint on AI buildout. The IEA projects global data-center electricity use rising from ~415 TWh in 2024 (~1.5% of total) toward ~945 TWh by 2030 (roughly 4×).
- Data-center siting, water use, and grid strain are emerging as local political flashpoints. Public opinion favors data-center approaches that protect consumers from high electricity costs and environmental impact.
- Grid capacity, power procurement, and cooling are now first-order bottlenecks alongside chip supply.

## Political
- The federal posture is deregulatory and pro-preemption. A Dec 11, 2025 executive order ("Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI," EO 14365) directs a DOJ litigation task force (active Jan 10, 2026) to challenge state AI laws, a Commerce review of "burdensome" state laws, and an FTC policy stance; it exempts child safety, AI compute/data-center infrastructure, and state procurement from preemption.
- In March 2026 the White House released a National Policy Framework urging Congress to enact broad preemption under a light-touch standard.
- AI is politically "up for grabs" — voters trust neither party to handle it well; negative sentiment concentrates among younger voters and women under 50.
- A notable administration reversal: driven by national-security concerns over advanced model capabilities, the administration is reportedly considering pre-release frontier-model evaluation requirements, and CAISI (the renamed US AI Safety Institute) announced evaluation partnerships with Google, Microsoft, and xAI — a shift for an administration that entered opposing AI oversight.

## Legal / Regulatory
- Preemption is NOT settled law. A 10-year state-law moratorium failed in the Senate 99-1 (July 2025). State laws remain enforceable unless/until preempted by statute or struck down by courts. Prudent posture is to keep complying with state law while monitoring.
- California SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier AI Act) is in effect as of Jan 1, 2026: frontier developers (>10^26 FLOPS) must publish risk frameworks, report safety incidents, and provide whistleblower protections; penalties up to $1M/violation for firms over $500M revenue. Also effective: AB 2013 (training-data transparency); SB 942 (AI-content disclosure/watermarking, delayed to Aug 2, 2026).
- Texas TRAIGA (HB 149) in force Jan 1, 2026 — scaled back from an EU-style draft to prohibiting specific harmful practices, intent-based liability, AG enforcement with a 60-day cure period.
- Colorado's comprehensive SB 24-205 never took effect; in May 2026 it was repealed/replaced by the narrower SB 26-189 (ADMT-focused), effective Jan 1, 2027. It was the only state law named in the Dec 2025 EO.
- New York amended the RAISE Act (March 2026) to mirror SB 53 (revenue-based threshold), easing multistate compliance.
- A bipartisan federal discussion draft — the Great American AI Act (June 4, 2026, Obernolte/Trahan, 269 pages) — proposes the first comprehensive federal framework in exchange for a 3-year freeze on state laws regulating how models are *built* (not how they're *used*). Not yet introduced or voted on.
- State legislative volume is high: ~1,561 AI bills introduced across 45 states as of March 2026, surpassing all of 2024.
- EU AI Act: GPAI obligations took effect Aug 2, 2025; high-risk obligations have an Aug 2, 2026 deadline, though the Commission proposed (Nov 2025) delaying high-risk implementation toward 2027. It remains the binding global benchmark.

## Security / Geopolitical
- State-linked misuse of frontier AI is documented and rising. In June 2026, OpenAI disclosed PRC-linked influence operations using ChatGPT to amplify US domestic grievances — notably targeting the data-center/electricity-cost debate — rated low-impact (Category One) but notable for testing narratives against AI infrastructure itself.
- US–China competition centers on compute: export controls on advanced chips and high-bandwidth memory; Chinese stockpiling ahead of restrictions; Chinese model developers (DeepSeek and others) demonstrating competitive capability at lower cost.
- Frontier cyber capability is operationalizing: documented cases of AI-assisted/AI-orchestrated cyber operations and the first AI-developed exploit code; labs now rate some models "High" in cyber capability.
- National-security concern over advanced model capabilities is reshaping US oversight posture (see Political) — government model-review arrangements with major labs are now in place.
- Critical-infrastructure exposure (power grid, financial systems) to AI-enabled attacks is a growing institutional concern.

## Demographic
- Attitudes split sharply by group. Data for Progress (Feb 2026): men favorable +16, women unfavorable -10; under-45 favorable +25, 45+ unfavorable -10; Black voters +29 and Latino +10 favorable, white voters -3.
- Generational pessimism is strongest among the young despite high usage: Quinnipiac found Gen Z 36% support / 58% oppose AI, the most negative of any generation; NBC found 18-34s at net -44 favorability and women 18-49 at -41.
- A usage/education divergence at work: among employed college-educated voters, daily AI use jumped from 22% (Aug 2025) to 34% (early 2026), while non-college use fell ~6 points — a widening adoption gap.
- Overall adoption keeps rising: ~56-66% report using an AI tool in recent months; professional/managerial and white-collar workers adopt far faster than blue-collar workers and retirees.
